did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically conventional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transfer learning architectures (ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB4), to perform multi-class skin lesion classification and early detection of skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. It also employed Squeeze-and-Excitation attention modules, Focal Loss, and Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for feature extraction and interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, including the training and evaluation of deep learning models (Custom CNN, ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB4) on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, AUC-ROC, recall), describes a methodology involving data preprocessing and augmentation, and discusses new findings regarding model interpretability using Grad-CAM. It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: Deep Learning Models-Based Skin Cancer Classification and Early Detection
brief_summary: This study systematically compared conventional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with transfer learning models (ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB4) for multi-class skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. To address class imbalance and enhance interpretability, the research integrated Squeeze-and-Excitation attention modules, Focal Loss, and an improved Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) technique. Experimental results demonstrated that transfer learning models outperformed custom CNNs, with ResNet50 achieving 77.11% accuracy and EfficientNetB4 attaining the highest AUC-ROC of 0.943 while using significantly fewer parameters. Despite these advancements, rare lesion categories such as dermatofibroma showed persistently low recall rates, highlighting ongoing challenges with minority class learning. The enhanced Grad-CAM method improved localization accuracy by 18.7% compared to conventional implementations, offering clearer insights into model decision patterns. These findings highlight the potential of efficient transfer learning architectures combined with interpretability enhancements to advance AI-assisted dermatological diagnostics.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion classification (7 classes: akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'multi-class skin lesion classification' using the HAM10000 dataset. Models (Custom CNN, ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB4) predict discrete diagnostic categories (lesion types) encoded as numerical labels 0-6. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, F1 score, recall, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, introduced by Tschandl et al. It contains 10,015 dermoscopic images covering 7 types of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (Custom CNN, ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB4) with a 70% split.
use.validation: Used for validation with a 20% split to monitor performance and apply early stopping.
use.testing: Used for final model evaluation with a 10% split.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Addressing class imbalance in the dataset to improve recall for minority classes (e.g., melanoma).
Quantifying the overlap between model-generated heatmaps and ground truth to evaluate interpretability and localization precision.
Monitoring model performance during training to prevent overfitting and determine optimal stopping points (early stopping implied by validation loss monitoring).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, recall, and AUC-ROC without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: Transfer learning models outperformed custom CNNs. EfficientNetB4 achieved the highest AUC-ROC (0.943) while using 4.3 times fewer parameters than ResNet50. Rare lesion category dermatofibroma (df) showed persistently low recall (≤ 25%, specifically 0% for CNN and VGG16). Enhanced Grad-CAM improved localization accuracy by 18.7% compared to conventional implementations. Focal Loss (γ=2, α=0.75) improved melanoma recall to 45.05% compared to 28.83% with standard cross-entropy loss.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50, VGG16, and EfficientNetB4 used ImageNet-pretrained weights as feature extractors. Custom CNN trained from scratch.

Custom CNN.blocks: Four convolutional blocks, each with two convolutional layers and a max pooling layer
Custom CNN.attention: Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) attention module inserted after each convolutional block

ResNet50.modification: Backbone frozen initially; custom classification head with SE attention mechanism added

VGG16.modification: Backbone frozen initially; custom classification head with SE attention mechanism added

EfficientNetB4.modification: Backbone frozen initially; custom classification head with SE attention mechanism added

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layers with softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied ±15° rotations, horizontal flipping, and random cropping to enhance model generalization.
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) was used to enhance texture details, particularly in dermoscopic images.

conclusions: Transfer learning models (ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB4) outperformed conventional custom CNNs in multi-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset.
ResNet50 achieved the highest accuracy of 77.11% among the tested models.

EfficientNetB4 achieved the highest AUC-ROC of 0.943 while using significantly fewer parameters (19 million) compared to ResNet50 (82 million), making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.
The integration of Focal Loss (γ=2, α=0.75) effectively addressed class imbalance, improving melanoma recall to 45.05% compared to 28.83% with standard cross-entropy loss.

Rare lesion categories, specifically dermatofibroma (df), exhibited persistently low recall rates (≤ 25%), highlighting ongoing challenges with minority class learning despite the use of advanced loss functions.
The enhanced Grad-CAM method improved localization accuracy by 18.7% compared to conventional implementations, providing clearer insights into model decision patterns and enhancing interpretability.

EfficientNetB4 demonstrated superior parameter efficiency and stable learning trajectories, offering a viable balance between diagnostic performance and computational feasibility for real-world deployment.

Future work should explore few-shot learning approaches and synthetic data generation to better support underrepresented lesion categories, as well as the integration of vision transformers with CNN backbones to capture global context.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (Nevus at 66.9%), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or datasets.
No external validation was performed; results are confined to the internal 70/20/10 split of the HAM10000 dataset.
Formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was not reported; performance comparisons are based on descriptive metrics.
Rare lesion categories, specifically dermatofibroma (df), showed persistently low recall (≤ 25%, with 0% for Custom CNN and VGG16), indicating poor performance on minority classes despite Focal Loss.
Melanoma recall remained suboptimal (45.05% for ResNet50), which is a critical limitation for a diagnostic tool where false negatives have severe clinical consequences.
The Custom CNN showed signs of overfitting, with training accuracy >90% but validation accuracy plateauing at 70-80%.
VGG16 achieved a notably low AUC-ROC of 0.479, suggesting poor discriminative ability compared to other models.
The paper does not report specific counts for the training, validation, and testing splits, only the percentages (70/20/10).
Deployment feasibility is highlighted for EfficientNetB4 due to its parameter efficiency (19M vs 82M for ResNet50), but clinical relevance is limited by the lack of real-world prospective validation.
